items.description: Resumes missing key contact info or including full street addresses can risk privacy or rejection.
items.tip: Add your name, city, active phone, and professional email — plus relevant links like LinkedIn or portfolio.

items.description: Jumping straight into sections without context fails to communicate your value quickly.
items.tip: Write a 2–3 line summary showing your skills, achievements, and what you’ll bring to the company.

items.description: Non-essential sections consume valuable resume space and distract from strengths.
items.tip: Omit hobbies and references unless specifically requested by the employer.

items.mistake: Ignoring or Misplacing the Skills Section
items.description: Poor placement or irrelevant skills can confuse both recruiters and ATS scans.
items.tip: Place a concise 5-skill section near the top for technical jobs or near the bottom for general roles.

items.description: Uneven margins, font variations, or bolding inconsistencies make the resume appear unprofessional.
items.tip: Use consistent formatting, uniform alignment, and two font styles maximum — no graphics or colors.

items.description: Listing duties instead of achievements makes you blend in with every other applicant.
items.tip: Start each bullet with a powerful verb and quantify impact with numbers or business outcomes.

items.description: Leaving out volunteer work, freelance, or training projects removes valuable proof of skill.
items.tip: Include all unpaid but relevant roles that demonstrate transferable or applied abilities.

items.description: Generic resumes fail both human reviewers and AI-driven applicant systems.
items.tip: Tailor every resume using keywords from each job description for ATS compatibility.

items.description: Including obsolete software or irrelevant personal skills weakens your resume.
items.tip: Add only current, in-demand tools and languages relevant to your target role.

items.mistake: Creating a Resume That’s Too Long or Short
items.description: Overloaded resumes lose focus; short ones lack impact and metrics.
items.tip: Keep one page for students, two pages for experienced candidates with 10+ years.

items.description: Overcrowded text or poor spacing reduces readability and recruiter engagement.
items.tip: Use clean white space, clear section breaks, and modern fonts like Roboto or Calibri.

items.description: Mentioning expected pay early shows poor understanding of hiring etiquette.
items.tip: Discuss salary expectations only during final interview stages, not on your resume.

items.description: Overstuffed phrases like 'go-getter' or difficult jargon confuse recruiters.
items.tip: Use clear, actionable language and demonstrate skills with quantifiable results.

items.description: Resumes listing only job tasks fail to show your measurable impact.
items.tip: Convert duties into achievement-based lines — highlight metrics and outcomes.

items.description: Emails like ‘coolguy123’ undermine credibility instantly.
items.tip: Use your first and last name for a professional email ID.

items.description: Generic bullet points make it impossible to measure your true performance.
items.tip: Include numbers, percentages, or timelines that highlight scope and improvement.

items.description: JPG or PNG formats cannot be processed by ATS and appear unprofessional.
items.tip: Submit resumes in PDF or DOCX format with proper naming conventions (e.g., Name_Resume2025.pdf).

items.description: Incorrect phone or email details cause recruiters to discard applications.
items.tip: Verify all contact details before submission — including city, number, and email.

items.description: AI scanners reject resumes missing terms used in the job post.
items.tip: Extract key phrases from job descriptions and embed them naturally in your text.

items.mistake: Adding a Picture or Graphical Ratings
items.description: Photos or graph-based skill meters often block ATS reading.
items.tip: Remove decorative visuals and describe skill levels using text (e.g., ‘Advanced’, ‘Intermediate’).

items.description: Poorly named files look careless and are harder to identify.
items.tip: Name your file clearly — e.g., 'Kishan_Mistry_Resume.pdf'.

items.description: Sentences like 'responsible for' lack ownership and leadership.
items.tip: Begin each bullet with active verbs like ‘Built’, ‘Implemented’, or ‘Delivered’.

items.description: Missing years or institution names makes your credentials seem incomplete.
items.tip: Include degree, university, year of completion, and CGPA if recent.

items.description: Typos or uneven design reflect poor attention to detail.
items.tip: Manually review and use proofreading tools before uploading.

items.description: Free, outdated templates may break in ATS systems or look cluttered.
items.tip: Use optimized templates from platforms like Canva, Novoresume, or Resume.io.
